rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Establishing the Rights of Marginalized Indigenous Tamang Children through Culturally Sensitive Learning
rows.description:
There are several ethnic groups and indigenous people in Nepal, and the country has internationally committed to improve the rights of indigenous people. The tamang language is part of Tibetan Burman languages, and it’s among the five most spoken languag
es in Nepal. Implementing linguistic rights has been a huge challenge for Nepal, in which over 100 languages are spoken and in which structural reforms are still ongoing. Even though time has been reserved for education of both Nepalese and a local languag
e, English is often taught instead of a local language due to lack of linguistic knowledge and negative attitudes. The project continues directly from a project previously funded by KIOS. The aim is to promote right to education on one’s mother tongue am
ongst children speaking Tamang language. Also the continuation of the project seeks to enhance tamang-speaking children’s cultural and educational rights in schools as well as inform about linguistic and cultural rights to the larger public. Childspace F
oundation Nepal was founded in 2003 by democracy and human rights activists belonging in tamang linguistic minority and indigenous population. The organization was originally registered as Helping Society Nepal in 2003, but the name was changed into Childs
pace Foundation Nepla in 2011. The organisation’s activities focus especially on improving the status and rights of tamang minority and, on a larger scale, on enhancing the rights of indigenous people and ethnic minorities. www.childspacefoundation.org

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Nepal Development Programme
rows.description:
Nepal Development Programme aims at improving food insecurity, sustainable livelihoods and gender equality as well as decease caste/ethnicity-based discriminations, mitigate disaster and climate change impacts and empower towards enforcement of human right
s as well as participation in decision-making at local and national context. Programme covers 30 Village Development Committees (VDCs). Expected results:• Enhanced capacity of disadvantaged communities to reduce disaster risk and to prepare for, respond 
to and, when necessary recover from emergencies and disasters.• Increased income and food security attained by all members within disadvantaged households.• Increased capacity of cooperatives in enhancing the sustainable livelihoods of their membership
, with particular focus on the poorer segments.• Increased participation, voice and influence of priority populations in local institutions and decision-making processes. Rights-holders /beneficiaries: Marginalised, vulnerable and conflict-affected peopl
e such as Dalit, freed bonded labourers, disaster affected population, ethnic minorities, landless, women-headed households and childrenImplementing partner:    The Lutheran World Federation Nepal (LWF Nepal).

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Actions for Advancing Freedom of Freed Haliyas, Kamaiyas and Kamlaharis
rows.description:
Freed Haliyas, ex-Kamaiyas and Kamlaris (HaKK) have not yet fully realised the true sense of freedom. They are aware but still lacking desired level of empowerment. Thousands of right-holders are still denied their rights and entitlements. Therefore, right
-holders need to be further supported to empower them to claim and enjoy their rights. The duty-bearers require enough support in formulation policies, strategies, guidelines and implement those aiming the just rehabilitation of freed HaKK. The project, th
us, focuses on livelihood, education, capacity building and advocacy to facilitate duty-bearers’ and right-holders’ efforts to end this bondage system permanently. The overall goal of the project is to enhance the dignified living of freed Haliyas, Ex-
 Kamaiyas and Kamlaris. The project aims at advancing socio-economic condition of freed Haliya, ex- Kamaiyas and Kamlaris enhancing their access to livelihood assets and promoting equality and non-discrimination Expected results:• Increased access of the
 rights-holders (i.e. freed Haliyas, ex-Kamaiyas and Kamlaris), their groups and associations to services and resources, and fulfillment of their rights • Enhanced access of the rights-holders to resilient livelihood and employment opportunities • Incr
eased access of children and youth among the rights-holders to uninterrupted quality education • Improved harmony and relations between freed bonded labourers and their ex-mastersRights-holders /beneficiaries: Freed Haliyas, ex- Kamaiyas and Kamlaris.Imp
lementing partner:  The Lutheran World Federation Nepal (LWF Nepal)

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Equality and Non-Discrimination for Sustainable Peace (ENDS)
rows.description:
Dalits continue to be among the poorest, under educated and under-represented at the political level. The project will raise awareness on unlawful and inhuman practices of caste-based discrimination and untouchability among both Dalit and non-Dalit populat
ion as well as prepare them to speak up against these practices; educate and sensitise government authorities on the anti-discrimination legal provisions and measures. The project will mobilise human rights defenders for supporting victims and empower them
 to seek justice through formal process, and educate and empower women and youths to engage in peacebuilding process in their community. This will contribute for the protection and promotion of fundamental human rights and freedom of Dalit, women and other
 marginalised groups in Nepal.Expected results:• Enhanced voices and roles of Dalit and civil society organisations in encouraging government to implement non-discriminatory policies and equality frameworks contributing to peace• Enhanced participation
 and role of marginalised women and youths in peace building in the community with their increased access and linkage to educational and livelihood opportunities• Increased engagement of religious, social and political leaders in peacebuilding and social
 harmony dealing with injustice and discriminationRights-holders /beneficiaries:  Dalit women and youth Implementing partner:  Nepal National Dalit Social Welfare Organization (NNDSWO)

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Grassroots Dialogues and Sustainable rehabilitation and reconstruction
rows.description:
Program I 1. Grassroots and National level dialogues will be organised under the Social Forum banner. SADED, through its grassroots dialogue, collects memories, thoughts and experience from the grassroots level 2. Those interesting and important memories a
nd outcomes of the dialogues will be published and disseminated through newsletters, newspaper articles, blogs and websites. 3. SADED-Nepal prioritizes to interact with various social movements in South Asia. Since it is easy for Nepalese to visit Pakistan
, and Bangladesh in comparison to Indians, SADED-Nepal will engage with social movements there so that regional social movements especially Social Forum will be strengthened. Program II: Sustainable rehabilitation and reconstruction (after 2015 earthquake)
 1. To stay with our green principles to use local technological skills and material for reconstruction: There is a risk that some of the multinational companies will monopolize the reconstruction discourse in order to expand their capital. SADED-Nepal has
 a plan to recruit some of the technicians to mobilize in the affected areas before and during the reconstruction work. They will be given chance to have exposure visits to places like Uttarakhand and Assam of India. These technicians will be able to sugge
st a good engineered structures so that the reconstructed houses with local materials will be equally or even more stronger than that of corporate promoted "modern" houses. Dialogues will be organized in the affected areas with the participation of communi
ty-based organizations and local elites. Also we will invite experts from Uttarakhand and Assam for sharing dialogues in the affected areas. 2. Resuming/Building/supporting public spaces for children and elders in the villages: SADED-Nepal have identified 
5 Village Development Communities (VDCs) in Nuwakot and Rasuwa/Sindupalchowk from where we start relief and reconstruction work of children's space. While doing so, pre-primary schools, play schools along with the village based public spaces will be recons
tructed where it is destroyed. We will take a VDC, and after having need assessments in the villages, will help reconstruct damaged infrastructures so that public spaces can resume/start their activities. Elderly and young people will be involved in runnin
g these children spaces. Furthermore, midday meals and play materials will be provided in a school and a children’s play space.

rows.reporting: Nuffic
rows.title: Sexual and reproductive health courses for school-aged children (8-19 years of age) in Nepal

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Accountable and Inclusive Schools
rows.description:
Inclusive and Accountable Schools is a cooperation project of Loo Niva Child Concern Patan Community Based Rehabilitation and Interpedia Finland Ry. The project running from 2017 to 2020 works in South Lalitpur which suffers from a low rate of public schoo
ls and was hard hit by the earthquakes in the spring 2015. This project builds on previous co-operation between Loo Niva and Interpedia with a new partnership with Patan CBR adding expertise in children with disabilities.Previous work aimed to contribute t
o strengthening efficient and effective school and local education governance mechanism in South Lalitpur. This project is focused on promoting inclusive and accountable schools as well as advancing the realization of the right to education for marginalize
d children who face discrimination particularly due to disabilities or the caste but also due to gender and ethnicity. The goal to increase inclusion of these vulnerable groups of children will be achieved by identifying and finding excluded children; prov
iding material and financial support that sustains enrolment to school; sensitizing training and rising awareness about rights needs and potential of marginalized children among children parents and teachers; improving school governance and using local med
ia for raising awareness of the importance of education for all children. The three organizations involved have versatile expertise of both advancing the right to education improving governance and advancing the involvement of children from margin groups f
or instance children with disabilities and dalit children. Moreover the project has been designed with consultation of children local communities and authorities for ensuring engagement of the local communities as well as the most effective implementation 
of the project in promoting the right to education. From the beginning the project has been planned in collaboration with local and national level authorities other NGOs and community groups for ensuring the sustainability and continuity of the good result
s of the project.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Unlocking doors to education for children with disabilities related to hearing and vision in Bara district Nepal
rows.description:
In Nepal children are not screened for visual or hearing impairments. Knowledge of eye- and ear health is generally poor. Traditional beliefs and treatments can be detrimental to sight and vision. Having a disability is still greatly stigmatised. Although 
the majority of children in Nepal have access to education differently abled children are still mostly excluded from it. This is a major risk for marginalisation. The project which is a 2-year pilot in Bara district in the Terai region in the south of Nepa
l aims to increase the enrollment of 3 to 8-year-old children with vision or hearing impairment in public preschools. The inhabitants of the district are poor and the number of children not attending school is considerable. The majority of pupils in Bara a
re boys and the project aims to promote girls' right to education.Grass-root level volunteers are educated to identify children with problems with vision or hearing. The children identified through screening are examined at eye + ear camps to diagnose cond
itions affecting sight and vision. It is estimated that most of these children have problems that can easily be cured with simple treatments. Children with a severe visual or hearing disability will undergo a 4-6 month rehabilitation to enable life outside
 home and attending school. Awareness raising campaigns regarding eye- and ear health as well as rights of disabled children will be organised among local inhabitants during the camps.This project is as a collaboration between a Nepalese NGO B.P. Eye Found
ation and PSR Finland. The collaboration was initiated from Nepal. Like the previous work of B.P. Eye Foundation this project aims to screen for and promote treatment of disabling conditions as well as contribute to a change in the Nepalese view on the rig
hts of people with disabilities remove barriers and stigmas associated with disabilities and enable children with disabilities to become productive citizens. The Department of Education (DoED) Nepal has signed a Memorandum of Understanding on implementing 
these pilot protocols in other districts in Nepal.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Mother Tongue Based Early Childhood Education Program into Lhomi Language in Nepal
rows.description:
Lhomi Preschool Program in Nepal is a liaison between Wycliffe Finland (WRK) and its partner Nepal Lhomi Society (NELHOS). The main output will be Mother Tongue Based Early Childhood Education program for the Lhomi.The status of children in Nepal is often 
very poor especially among indigenous people groups. Lhomi children are often discriminated. There is no preschools available and they must start primary school in Nepali language which is unknown for them. This will have a bad effect to their success in t
he school and finally it causes exclusion.Providing pre-school opportunity by their mother tongue this project will increase parity in the education levels of children in the target communities with other children in the country. Children's rights will be 
increased by providing them pre-school education that equip them and easy their transition to the first grade in Nepali instructed primary school. In doing so children will comprehend and learn faster they will stay in school and ultimately will increase t
heir self-development. This will be achieved through a well-founded Mother Tongue Based Early Childhood Education (MTB ECE) -program that will be developed in the target language.The project aims to reduce inequality and vulnerability of indigenous childre
n. The rights of girls disabled children and low cast children will be emphasized. Moreover gender equality reduction of inequality disability and climate sustainability will be part of the learning material.NELHOS is responsible of project implementation.
 As an organization they will gain capacity of mother tongue education as well as they become strong advocate of minority language groups and the rights of marginalized groups in Nepal.Main beneficiaries are the Lhomi language speaking children their paren
ts and the local education actors who benefit of increased capacity. The government supports mother tongue education and pre-schools but often they do not have resources to start schools in the remote areas.Key activities:- Influencing the attitudes of the
 stakeholders.- Producing MTB ECE program and material for it.- Training teachers.- Pilot classes.- Capacity building.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Advancing Resilience and Empowerment
rows.description:
The UN Women implemented project Advancing Resilience and Empowerment ARE is part of the annual work plan for the year 2017 of UN Women's Nepal Country Office. The project is being implemented in the course of 2017 and its total budget is 400.000 EUR. The 
ARE project is divided into two result areas. The first result area aims to strengthen the capacity of duty bearers (authorities and government) to integrate gender equality objectives into policies and plans. The project provides technical expertise in su
pport of drafting Nepal's second NAP and key national legislation with relevance for advancing gender equality obligations enshrined in the constitution. The project also provides technical support to mainstream gender equality and social inclusion objecti
ves into post-earthquake reconstruction planning. The second result area focuses on strengthening the ability of excluded women's groups for economic and social participation. The beneficiaries are provided with vocational and entrepreneurship training as 
well as counseling in disaster preparedness training and humanitarian crisis management. As for the first results area the activities benefit the entire country as the focus is on national level plans and policies. The second results area focuses on 1600 b
eneficiaries comprised of girls and women from crisis affected or marginalized groups.The ARE project is a continuation of the Finnish-funded SIWPSAN project which supported the drafting and implementation of Nepal's first National Action Plan on UN securi
ty council resolutions 1325 and 1820. The project is currently being evaluated and the results are very promising; some of the targets have even been exceeded. The Ministry for Women Children and Social Welfare and the Ministry for Peace and Reconstruction
 has taken up the project activities as best practices in the implementing the National Action Plans.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: Business linkage programme (Finnpartnership)
rows.description: Long term establishment in the target country (e.g. subsidiary, representative office, maintenance company) related to Basic metal industries

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Action Plan for implementation of UPR Recommendations
rows.description:
The project content for 2017 will be defined later on; it will be a continuum for 2015-2016. The budget will be allocated from 11591 “Promotion of Global Justice”. The description for 2015-2016: The Overall Objective of the project is to promote and pr
otect human rights of Dalit in Nepal. The Specific Objective is to contribute for addressing the key human rights concerns of Dalit through second cycle of Universal Periodic Review (UPR). The specific human rights issues and concerns of Dalit community di
scussed and documented through a participatory process with the involvement of more than 200 Dalit organizations representing all five development regions of Nepal. A report on the specific human rights issues and concerns of Dalit will be developed and su
bmitted to UPR concerned committee of UN within third week of March 2015. The specific human rights concerns of Dalit advocated and lobbied with the delegates of different countries during UPR of Nepal in October / November 2015.The project will carry out 
a quick research on the implementation status of UPR recommendations in Nepal, Regional level consultation workshop will be organized at Nepalganj, Chitwan and Itahari, National level consultation workshop will be held at Kathmandu. Advocacy and lobbying w
ill be carried out with international governments through embassies in Kathmandu, Advocacy and lobbying will be done to the government of Nepal for recommendation involving media houses.  Lobbying to the HRC members and other state representatives during i
nteractive dialogue session will be carried out.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Empower youth with disabilities through information access from youth disability information center establishment in Kathmandu
rows.description: Youth with disabilities will establish an information center in Kathmandu. The center will disseminate information about human rights issues, disability laws and policies, scholarships, training and work opportunities.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Computer Training for Employment to the Disabilities
rows.description: Ten unemployed visually impaired people will take a six-months long training course in computer technology. After the training they will be employed as computer teachers, computer operators or they will run computer cyber cafes.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Micro Enterprise Development for Self Employment of PWDs of Rudrapur VDC
rows.description: The Foundation wants to establish an internet cafe with several services available, like internet surfing, printing, scanning, photocopier, lamination, nepali/english typing etc. The cafe will employ at least 3 persons with disabilities.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Computer course for board members of DAN who use sign language and have low vision
rows.description: Teaching computer skills to deafblind people to enable them to communicate, access the internet, build organizational capacity and find employment.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Improving Gender Equality of BVI Women
rows.description:
BVI is an association of blind and visually impaired women. They want to advocate for the rights of BVI women by providing organizational management training, training on gender roles among spouses, personality development and mobility training to BVI wome
n, and strategy development training for the association

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Digitalizing accounting system for economic transparency by developing management skill for PWDs
rows.description:
This is a cooperative of PWDs that plans to digitalize their bookkeeping system and increase capacity. Three executives will learn how to use the sofware to be purchased, the group will purchase a computer, and 15 people will learn how to run the loans and
 collection scheme.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Developing the access of persons with intellectual disability in education and enhancing the rights of learning and entertainment
rows.description:
Parents with Intellectually Disabled Children (PWID) will start a six-months education project for their children. Five children will also learn how to make paper products like envelopes. The project will also develop accessible learning material for intel
lectually disabled children.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Economic upliftment of Persons with Intellectual Disabilities and their parents through production and selling of spices
rows.description: An association of parents of people with intellectual disabilities will start producing spices. They will purchase machines and raw material, select the beneficiaries of the project, draft a marketing strategy and start the production.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Capacity building of persons with disability
rows.description:
The organization will organize a vocational training course for five women with disabilities. The other activities of the project are related to capacity building of the organization: formation of 4 Self Help Groups, leadership training, and proposal and r
eport writing training. The goal is to enable the self help groups to apply for local grants. The organization will also celebrate International Day of People with Disabilties on 3 December.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Empowering Blind Persons in Morang District
rows.description:
Morang Association of the Blind (MAB) will provide organizational management training, and advocacy and lobbying skills training to their membership. MAB will also provide musical training and vocational training like mushroom production and goat rearing. 
The intention is for the trainees to apply for local government money to start working after training.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Inclusion and independence of persons with congenital deafblindness in Nepal
rows.description:
Congenitally deafblind people will be tought activites of daily living (personal hygiene, dressing, eating etc.), and basic living skills (interacting with other people). The group will produce video clips of deafblind people´s situation to raise awarenes
s about deafblindness. A workshop for concerned government authorities and and other DPOs will be organized. Nepal is due to submit a report on the implementation of UNCRPD, and the group will contribute to a shadow report by giving information about the s
ituation of deafblind people.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Livelihood support and advocacy program for visually impaired
rows.description:
The project has two major activities. The first activity is to organize a one-day interactive workshop , the topics being inclusion of blind and visually impaired people into society. The second activity is vocational training. Ten visually impaired person
s will learn to make liquid soap and Nim powder.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Income generation program for persons with disabilities
rows.description:
This is a vocational training project. The members of the group are taught either manufacturing sweet boxes, keeping small shops, rearing goats or making bamboo products. The local District Village Committee (local government) will provide seed money for t
he trained people (with the exception of bamboo crafts because they can collect bamboo for free) so they can start working.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Dialogues for ensuring CPRD principles in the forthcoming Disability Bill in Nepal
rows.description:
The Office of Abilis Local Expert in Nepal wants to arrange a series of ten dialogues with nepalese parliamentarians and DPOs to ensure that the now tabled Nepal Disability Bill will include human rights of persons with disabilities, in line with the UNCRP
D.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Basic Computer Training to Blind Persons in Far-West Region
rows.description:
25 blind students will take a 75 days long basic computer training course to be able to utilize electronic learning material in their studies. 50 blind students will take a two-day course to learn how to use their Android devices to access internet, e-mail
 and electronic books.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Economic Empowerment of PWDs and Local Resource Mobilization
rows.description:
The applicant organization was established by Women with Disabilities. The project plan is to provide leadership training for 30 leaders of local Self-Help-Groups, conduct an awareness raising campaign by broadcasting in FM radio, and to provide vocational
 training (goat rearing for 10 women, wood carving for five men).

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Enterpreneurship development of Deaf Women and promotion of sign language
rows.description:
DAR is planning to implement four activities: 1) Training on doll making for hearing impaired women, 15 days 2) Collecting local signs to be added to the national sign language dictionary of Nepal) 3) Training on inclusive education to 25 Deaf leaders and 
parents of Deaf children, 3 days 4) Setting up a showroom for showcasing the dolls made by the Deaf women.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Abilis Local Expert in Nepal 2016
rows.description:
Abilis Foundation covers the costs of the activities that occur when the Local Expert disseminates infomation about Abilis funding, facilitates groups to apply funding, monitors on-going projects and provides training and advice to applicant and implemneti
ng oragnizations. Activities are based on annual planning and are reported twice a year.

rows.reporting: Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland
rows.title: F.a: Human Rights Education for Promoting the Rights of Persons with Disabilities
rows.description:
People with disabilities experience in Nepal many forms of discrimination; Society's attitudes make it difficult to equal life, but also the institutional structures such as legislation and policy guidelines are often discriminatory towards people with dis
abilities. In addition to physical barriers, such as challenging routes may prevent people with disabilities from access to legitimate services. For those circumstances the disabled children are less attending the school than others. The project’s goal i
s to bring people with disabilities at the center of social development by promoting their human rights. The project aims to empower people with disabilities not only to demand their rights, but also to increase the awareness of the rights of disabled peop
le. In addition, the project seeks to influence political decision-makers, in order to the disabled people's rights be noted and the laws to be implemented. The project trains the members of the community and disability activists about human rights, partic
ularly about the rights of people with disabilities. In addition, there will be held discussions with representatives of the local district government, as well as eight events at the national level with policy makers in Kathmandu. CIL - Kathmandu began its
 work among disabled people's rights in 2005 and was officially registered in the following year. The organization 's name refers to the rights of the people with disabilities to make decisions and to get services that society should provide. The organizat
ion makes right -based work wirh issues considering people with disabilities and its primary activities include advocacy and inclusion of people with disabilities in political decision-making. The organization also provides advicing and training on issues 
related to persons with disabilities and informs the rights of disabled people . CIL – Kathmandu’s advocacy, together with some other organizations, has impacted on, that several ministries are now accessible communicated.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Rural Health Development Project
rows.description: Through its project planning and implementation approach RHDP has empowered community groups, especially women, and also influenced the village development committees (VDCs) to integrate community health demands in VDC planning, and thus contributed to local state building and better service delivery. After 17 years of cooperation, this will be the last phase of RHDP that will focus on consolidation and sustainability. As in the past, the project will aim at improving overall health status of people, especially of women, poor and disadvantaged groups in remote areas of Dolakha, Ramechhap and Okhaldhunga districts. Primary beneficiaries will be women, children, adolescents, and men, with special attention to Dalits and Janajatis.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: District Roads
rows.description: The Project Support Unit (PSU) supports the capacity building in the districts, for implementation of rural infrastructure and community development activities. The District Development Committees (DDC) and local groups implement the works, with support from the PSU. DoLIDAR is responsible for the Planning, Coordination, Fund flow and Monitoring of the Programme. SDC has inducted many good practices across the rural road projects in Nepal. The green roads concept has been adopted nationally. Until recently unheard issues of workers welfare are now being translated into policy, that include insurance, work safety, equal wage for equal work and facilities that help women to participate.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: NARC/CIMMYT Hill Maize Research Project
rows.description: SDC has been supporting HMRP since 1999. HMRP had very encouraging achievements in the first three phases, such as: i) setting of the maize research priorities, establishing participatory variety selection, validation and certification methodologies, linking research with extension for wider dissemination, ii) certification of nine improved varieties and production of 4,416 MT of improved maize seeds, and iii) linking farmers feedback to policy decisions through farmers assessment tests over released varieties. HMRP has contributed to establishing a national research system, linking research with extension and strengthening farmers groups to produce maize seeds and deal with markets for better and assured prices. This fourth phase will focus on strengthening seeds dissemination channels, developing market links, ensuring the decentralized seed quality assurance system and developing more new maize varieties as the demand remains high.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Trail Bridge Sub-Sector Program
rows.description: The Programme will bring economic benefits among poor communities, by improving access to markets and services. It also addresses poverty by generating off-farm employment of 2600 person days per bridge directly. It will ensure increased participation of disadvantaged groups and women in bridge building initiatives and empower them for better governance. As a result of these Swiss supports, a Sector Wide Approach is conceived by GoN with WB, ADB, DFID and SDC.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Trail Bridge Sub-Sector Program
rows.description: Areas of poor accessibility are strongly correlated with the incidence of poverty. Walking is still a major mode of mobility in remote places of Nepal. Moreover, in areas of new road construction, there is a growing demand for river crossings from populations alongside the road corridor. Swiss supported technical assistance for trail bridges construction will help enhance access to basic public services and economic opportunities by providing safe river crossings.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Homegarden Project
rows.description: Description indisponible pour cette activité

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Homegarden Project
rows.description: Small and marginal farmers in Nepal, especially women and children, are regularly exposed to malnutrition and are vulnerable to external shocks such as health risks and natural hazards. Home garden aims to improve family nutrition and reduce vulnerability of such individuals and households. Objectives are to diversify dietary sources of disadvantaged groups in homestead for family consumption and increase capacity for adaptation to natural hazards and climate change. The project strengthens the safety net and the coping strategy of the most vulnerable in Nepal.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Vegetable Seed Project
rows.description: Switzerland is recognizing the importance of high quality vegetable seed production through the promotion of private-public partnership among producer cooperatives, NGOs and private seed entrepreneurs in the seed sector. Producing seeds through farmers groups and cooperatives has been a successful approach, which allows a sustainable national seed production. The goal of VSP-III is to have improved food security and income for the poor and disadvantaged households in remote areas of Nepal. VSP-III will have two major focuses: 1) farm families from poor and DAGs produce and sell quality seeds; and 2) National Seed Board, Nepal Agriculture Research Council and Department of Agriculture enforce decentralised seed production and quality control through public and private institutions. VSP has proven synergies with other SDC supported agricultural projects, such as the Hill Maize Research Project in promoting seed related market and policy issues and the Home garden project in promoting diversification of vegetable production.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Vegetable Seed Project
rows.description: Switzerland is recognizing the importance of high quality vegetable seed production through the promotion of private-public partnership among producer cooperatives, NGOs and private seed entrepreneurs in the seed sector. Producing seeds through farmers groups and cooperatives has been a successful approach, which allows a sustainable national seed production. The goal of VSP-III is to have improved food security and income for the poor and disadvantaged households in remote areas of Nepal. VSP-III will have two major focuses: 1) farm families from poor and DAGs produce and sell quality seeds; and 2) National Seed Board, Nepal Agriculture Research Council and Department of Agriculture enforce decentralised seed production and quality control through public and private institutions. VSP has proven synergies with other SDC supported agricultural projects, such as the Hill Maize Research Project in promoting seed related market and policy issues and the Home garden project in promoting diversification of vegetable production.

rows.reporting: Nuffic
rows.title: Green roads for climate resilience and water management

rows.reporting: Nuffic
rows.title: Nature-Based Solutions for Climate Change Adaptation and Mitigation: Building Resilience for Municipalities in Nepal

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Decentralized Rural Infrastructure and Livelihood Project (DRILP)  Phase III
rows.description: DRILP Phase 3 will support the Government of Nepal to accelerate recovery and reconstruction following the devastating earthquake of 25 April 2015 and the major aftershock of 12 May 2015. The project will provide the Technical Assistance (TA) to implement the Asian Development Bank (ADB) financed Earthquake Emergency Assistance Project in rebuilding 450 kms of damaged roads in 12 districts[1] of Nepal and rehabilitate 200 kms of trails in two[2] districts. [1] The 12 earthquake affected project districts are Solukhumbu, Okhaldhunga, Ramechhap, Dolakha, Kavre, Sindhuli, Kathmandu, Lalitpur, Bhaktapur, Lamjung, Gorkha and Chitwan [2] Dolakha and Gorkha

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Local Infrastructure for Livelihood Improvement (LILI)
rows.description: Agricultural production, besides migration, will remain the main pillar for rural livelihood in Nepal for some time, mainly because suitable framework conditions for the other strategies (off-farm economic activities) will take time to evolve. Improving agricultural production requires reliable irrigation systems. In the context where big irrigation schemes usually receive attention, LILIs involvement in small irrigation provides meaningful contribution to improve the food security of the marginal farmers as well as help influence government policies in their favour.

rows.reporting: Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Netherlands
rows.title: DMV/HH - NP - 2007 - NRK
rows.description: humanitaire hulp in x-regio

rows.reporting: Gates Foundation
rows.title: Planning and Scoping grant: SAPLING Phase 2
rows.description: to support scoping, planning and design for next phase of South Asia Policy Leadership for Improved Nutrition and Growth (SAPLING) platform

rows.reporting: Gates Foundation
rows.title: Global HPV Burden Study
rows.description: To generate robust primary data of HPV burden among girls/women in LIC/LMICs in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa to inform intervention implementation and prioritization of R&D efforts that have the greatest potential public health impacts

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Local Governance and Community Development Programme (LGCDP) II
rows.description: As a result of this project, the demand side of local governance will be strengthened through the empowerment of right holders. Social mobilization interventions will enable beneficiaries to more effectively demand public services and hold national and local governments accountable. Simultaneously, the supply side will be strengthened. As a result, local bodies with strengthened institutional capacity and resources will provide services more effectively to the people, esp. to disadvantaged groups. In addition, an enabling policy environment will be promoted. Inter alia, this will help to prepare Nepal for a future federal system, as and when this system is defined in the new constitution.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Local Governance & Community Dev. Prog.
rows.description: LGCDP's over-arching goal is to contribute towards poverty reduction through inclusive responsive and accountable local governance and participatory community-led development that will ensure increased involvement of women, Dalits, Adibasi Janajatis, Muslims, Madhesis, disadvantaged groups in the local governance process. The programme will contribute towards attaining the goal through achieving its three major Programme Outcomes: (1) Citizens and communities empowered to exercise their voice and to engage actively with local governments on resource allocation and decision making; (2) Increased capacity of local governments to manage resources and deliver basic services in an inclusive and equitable manner; and (3) Strengthened policy and national institutional framework for decentralization, devolution and community development.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Supporting the BOGs Group Nepal
rows.description: The BOGs Group, which is co-chaired by Switzerland and the UN Resident Coordinator and comprises a number of international donors, has been functional since 2003. The BOGs, or Basic Operating Guidelines are the code of conduct under which these donors operate in the field, dispense aid and interact with authorities. Switzerland has taken the coordinative lead by funding the exchange of information and the maintenance of this common approach, and is renewing this commitment for an additional three years.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Safer Migration (SaMi) Project
rows.description: SaMi addresses the needs of Nepali men and women who migrate to the Middle East and Southeast Asia on short term labour contracts. To reduce the financial and social costs of migration and to increase its financial benefits, the project facilitates access to relevant information, pre-departure skills trainings, legal and psychosocial support. SaMI also strengthens governments capacity to effectively implement the labour migration policy and protect migrants. Some 170000 people will benefit from the project over four years.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: State Building at Local Level Project
rows.description: The ten-year long conflict (1996-2006) not only had a negative impact on the countries socio-economic development, but also destroyed and weakened many of its institutions including those at the local level: the village development committees (VDCs) and the district development committees (DDCs). The absence of elected VDC and DDC representatives since 2002 and a weak state have allowed political parties, private sector entrepreneurs and sometimes the administration itself to influence decisions and appropriate resources. SDC proposes to provide a modest budget support to the DDCs in two of its three focus districts: Khotang and Okhaldunga. Coupled with the current SDC presence and positioning in these districts, the budget support should allow SDC to increase its leverage and delivery of development outcomes. The project is also expected to foster a culture of dialogue (and control) among all stakeholders. This should build up experiences and skills that can be used by both the existing local administrations (and the political parties) to prepare for the upcoming transition towards a new federal state, and the new federal unit to possibly build upon. In this sense, the project is also expected to contribute to a smoother transition and prevent conflict.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Motorable Local Roads Bridge Programme (MLRBP)  Phase I
rows.description: The Department of Local Infrastructure Development and Agricultural Roads of the Ministry of Local Development (DoLIDAR/MoLD) is responsible for construction and maintenance of motorable bridges in addition to local roads but it has limited capacity to shoulder this responsibility. DoLIDAR is desirous of a) developing norms and standards appropriate to local roads and b) strengthening its capacities as well as that of the private sector so that these pilots can be replicated. In this regard, GoN has requested SDC to support them with technical assistance. Switzerland has gained significant recognition and visibility in Nepal over the last 4 decades through its engagement in the transport sector, particularly in the trail-bridge and local road sectors.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: River Protection Works Chitwan, Phase 1
rows.description: The main goal of the project is to safeguard the people and assets of eastern Chitwan from flood related disaster and improve their livelihoods and food security. Expected outputs (1): Local communities along Rapti River and in the project VDCs build and rehabilitate river protection works. (2): Local communities rehabilitate the irrigation canals originating from Rapti river. (3): Users committee and its sub committees formed to implement the works. (4): Communities, VDCs and the DDC strengthen their capacity to engage in dialogues with the central Government on the need to undertake larger integrated watershed management initiatives.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: National Forestry Programme
rows.description: The Goal is to contribute for improved livelihoods and resilience of poor men, women and children in Nepal. The Purpose is maximise the contribution of Nepals forestry sector to inclusive economic growth, poverty reduction and tackling climate change. Coverage will increase from 4 districts (current Swiss coverage) to 61 districts (out of 75 nationally) with full-fledged focused activities in 35 over the next 10 years. Focus is to expand poverty reduction and environmental outcomes of community forestry nationally through wider reach and deeper engagement at different levels.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Employment Fund, Phase II
rows.description: The earthquakes that struck Nepal in April and May 2015 caused the destruction of 500,000 houses and the damage of 280,000 houses[1]. Nepal does not have sufficient construction workers with knowledge of earthquake resilient technologies. This project will train 3,000 people from the disaster affected districts in reconstruction relevant trades and in the use of earthquake resilient building techniques while 4,000 houses, the trainees own as well as other homes, are built.     [1] Figures provided by the Government of Nepal: http://drrportal.gov.np

rows.title: Seed without borders (SWB): Regional cooperation and capacity building
rows.description: to support the mission of seed without borders in taking the next steps and building a regional consensus on diversification of seed varieties and capacity building of countries on seed certification

rows.reporting: Gates Foundation
rows.title: SAPLING 2.0
rows.description: To support South-South Cooperation showcasing Indian models of development for improved nutrition and growth and enable cross border technology exchange.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Power Plant Extension SCECO (PPE SCECO), Nepal  Single Phase
rows.description: The Power Plant Extension of SCECO will improve daily living condition and enhance economic opportunities by providing uninterrupted hydro-power supply to 1400 customers, and get up to 500 new connections to the local power grid, in the area of Salleri/Garma/Phalpu (HDI 0.45-0.50). Additionally, it will mitigate uncontrolled deforestation by reducing the use of firewood for cooking, heating and lighting purposes, in an area (lying between 2400  3000m alt.) where forest regeneration is slow. The project will thus enhance the sustainable use of local water resources while contributing to the maintenance of resilience to climate change at the local level.

rows.description: Schokland initiative

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Local Roads Improvement Programme (LRIP  I)
rows.description: Past SDC interventions have focussed on making rural road construction work for the poor and disadvantaged through labour-intensive construction methods. This project now takes proven approaches further by ensuring even more all-weather accessibility to markets and services by emphasising the importance of maintenance and preservation of built roads through promotion of labour-based roads maintenance methods and by fostering an institutional process in the districts, which should pave the way for the highly successful concepts of road construction and maintenance to be adopted as national standards.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Legal Aid to Improve People's Access to Criminal Defence Services
rows.description: Contribute to the development of an effective criminal legal aid system in Nepal that ensures access to justice for all women and men.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Rehabilitation of Critical Trail Bridges  Single Phase
rows.description: The lack of physical access to economic opportunities, natural resources and social services is highly correlated with incidence of poverty in Nepal. Social and economic vulnerability of disadvantaged groups is often exacerbated by reduced access to road network. One of the major reasons for constraints in rural Nepal is in-adequate or unsafe river crossings and lack of its maintenance. The proposed intervention will improve safe river crossings by rehabilitating the 137 critical trail bridges.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Reducing disabilities through a support to the Hospital and Rehabilitation centre for Disabled Children (HRDC)
rows.description: An estimate 5% of the population of Nepal is affected with physical disabilities and their livelihood is affected far beyond the physical handicap itself. They suffer from exclusion and social stigma, which in turn reduces their ability to be socially and economically integrated. The HRDC is since over 25 years the leading institution providing services to disabled children and will be able to treat some 15000 children per year with the equipment and mobile teams facilities made available through this programme.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Strengthening Local Governance Accountability Project (SLGAP)
rows.description: The project empowers communities to claim their developmental rights by capacitating local civil society organisations (CSOs) to hold their local governments accountable. This is achieved by applying various social accountability (SA) tools. A protracted phase of political transition, a lack of structural accountability and transparency, rising corruption at all levels of government, and an absence of elected representatives makes the intervention highly relevant for target communities in SDC project areas.

rows.reporting: Gates Foundation
rows.title: Supporting research in SEAR countries
rows.description: To improve retention in TB infection cascade of care in high-risk groups through a person-centered approach in the South-East Asian Region countries

rows.reporting: Gates Foundation
rows.title: Long-Term Follow-Up (LTFU) of OCV-S Phase 3 Study
rows.description: to support the delivery of oral cholera vaccine (OCV) in low- and middle-income countries by improving our understanding of the nature and longevity of immunity following vaccination with OCV.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Nepal Agricultural Markets Development Programme. Facilitating competitive rural production clusters and service markets in the hills and beyond
rows.description: Nepal Agricultural Markets Development Programme (NAM-DP) will sustainably increase the real income poor and disadvantaged women and men derive from participation in agricultural and interconnected goods, service and labour markets by addressing key constraints in market systems where the poor are present as entrepreneurs, workers and/or consumers. Interventions will focus on improving access to information, access to inputs, access to high-value output markets, and the enabling environment.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Gender-based and Domestic Violence Prevention and Response Programme (GDV Programme)
rows.description: Domestic and gender based violence (D/GBV) is widely prevalent in Nepal because of the dominance of patriarchal values, lack of awareness about rights, lack of support services and poor implementation of laws. Based on consultations with communities, the line ministry, the police and other donors, SDC will focus on working with civil society organizations and government agencies on awareness, prevention, legal aid and psychosocial support; and on strengthening duty bearers at district and national level, including policy implementation and reform.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Nepal Vocational Qualifications System (NVQS)
rows.description: In Nepal, 500000 youths enter the labour market yearly but remain un- or underemployed. The project will support the Ministry of Education to establish a National Vocational Qualifications Framework and a corresponding NVQ Authority to manage it. Learners and workers, especially from disadvantaged groups, will benefit from an improved vocational training qualification and skills certification system, thus increasing their chances for sustainable and decent employment.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Homegarden Project
rows.description: Small and marginal farmers in Nepal, especially women and children, are regularly exposed to malnutrition and are vulnerable to external shocks such as health risks and natural hazards. Home garden aims to improve family nutrition and reduce vulnerability of such individuals and households. Objectives are to diversify dietary sources of disadvantaged groups in homestead for family consumption and increase capacity for adaptation to natural hazards and climate change. The project strengthens the safety net and the coping strategy of the most vulnerable in Nepal.

rows.reporting: Gates Foundation
rows.title: Advocating, accelerating, and adapting DPI use case deployments
rows.description: To help low income, and low and middle income countries achieve faster socioeconomic growth by deploying digital public infrastructure at scale and help reach their sustainable development goals

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Nepal Agricultural Services Development Programme (NASDP)  Main Credit Phase 1
rows.description: Agriculture in Nepal is characterized by low growth and productivity, yet it still is the mainstay for 66% of the rural poor people. The small farmers will improve their productivity, income and food security thanks to the strengthening of a decentralized and pluralistic extension and research system, receptive to their needs and demands. Built on previous SDC interventions and in line with Nepals Agricultural Development Strategy, this system will also enhance their social inclusion.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Governance Facility (GF)
rows.description: People in Nepal still suffer from weak public accountability, lack of rule of law and prevailing impunity. Switzerland, Denmark and DFID have established a sector-wide multi-donor basket facility to support public and private institutions in the governance sector, aiming at improving the rule of law, access to justice for marginalized men and women, the protection of human rights and at strengthening public accountability mechanisms for the most vulnerable people in Nepal.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Enhanced Skills for Sustainable and Rewarding Employment (ENSSURE) (Main Credit)
rows.description: In Nepal, 450,000 youths enter the labour market yearly but remain un- or underemployed. Through ENSSURE, 21000 youths will increase their employability by attending company-based trainings such as apprenticeships and further training of workers, combined with off-the-job systematic courses[1].The strong collaboration between the Ministry of Education and industry associations will increase the skill level of workers; thus labor productivity and will lead  to more decent employment.       [1] Systematic courses could be: Pre-employment basics, day or block release for related trade theory, pre-assessment course, etc.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Decentralized Rural Infrastructure and Livelihood Project (DRILP)
rows.description: DRILP Phase 3 will support the Government of Nepal to accelerate recovery and reconstruction following the devastating earthquake of 25 April 2015 and the major aftershock of 12 May 2015. The project will provide the Technical Assistance (TA) to implement the Asian Development Bank (ADB) financed Earthquake Emergency Assistance Project in rebuilding 450 kms of damaged roads in 12 districts[1] of Nepal and rehabilitate 200 kms of trails in two[2] districts.[1] The 12 earthquake affected project districts are Solukhumbu, Okhaldhunga, Ramechhap, Dolakha, Kavre, Sindhuli, Kathmandu, Lalitpur, Bhaktapur, Lamjung, Gorkha and Chitwan[2] Dolakha and Gorkha

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Emergency Rehabilitation of Flood Damaged Trail Bridges  Single Phase
rows.description: The lack of physical access to economic opportunities, natural resources and social services is highly correlated with incidence of poverty. Devastations caused by natural disasters further exacerbate social and economic vulnerability of disadvantaged groups. Approximately 60000 people will regain their safe safe river crossings and their access to services through the rehabilitation and rebuilding of 21 strategic trail bridges damaged by the recent flash floods in August 2014 in 5 districts of Nepal.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Small Irrigation Prorgamme (SIP)
rows.description: Incidence of poverty in irrigated areas of Nepal is half that in rain-fed areas and access to irrigation water contributes thus to mitigate poverty. Since the marginal lands are better accessible through small irrigation schemes, reducing poverty of smallholder farmers through small irrigation systems[1] are more effective than medium-sized or large systems. SIP will not only help increase their agricultural productivity but will also enhance the capacity of national and local government so that they can respond to these needs.[1] Small-scale irrigation, defined by the GoN's irrigation policy as command areas smaller than 25 ha in the hills and mountains and 200 ha in Terai.

rows.reporting: Gates Foundation
rows.title: GTFCC Country Support Platform Interim Support
rows.description: to provide operational support to countries in the implementation of the "Ending Cholera - a Global Roadmap to 2030"

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Capacity-building for North Korea sanctions implementation in Latin America and Southeast Asia
rows.description: This project strengthens the implementation of UN Security Council Resolution (UNSCR) sanctions on North Korea in Latin America and Southeast Asia. Project activities include: (1) promoting open source monitoring and reporting to the private sector; (2) providing advanced counter-proliferation finance training in Mexico; (3) convening a counter-proliferation finance symposium for Central Banks and financial institutions in Southeast Asia; (4) building regional champions on counter-proliferation finance in the Caribbean and Latin America; (5) conducting an e-learning course on combatting North Korean proliferation finance; (6) providing webinars and in-person training sessions on counter-proliferation finance in Latin America; and (7) conducting interactive training on countering North Korean cyber threats for financial institutions in Costa Rica and Guatemala.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Motorable Local Roads Bridge Programme (MLRBP)
rows.description: MLRBP III will contribute directly to improved livelihoods of 1.2 million rural people through technical assistance in bridge building and facilitating improved access. Lack of access to services, markets and opportunities remains one of the reasons for high poverty. Bridges on local roads are critical for an all year round access for the rural poor, creating economic and social development opportunities for them, and reducing the huge economic costs they incur for travel, trade, health and farm inputs.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Motorable Local Roads Bridge Programme (MLRBP)
rows.description: Lack of access to services, markets and opportunities is one of the reasons for high incidence of poverty in Nepal. The Government of Nepal (GoN) has accorded a high priority to the construction of bridges to improve accessibility of remote areas in Nepal and increased the budget 5-fold in the last four years. The proposed phase will build on GoN’s confidence in the Swiss technical support, while preparing to accelerate the construction of bridges to facilitate improved access.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Support to Participatory Constitution Building in Nepal (SPCBN)
rows.description: The Constituent Assembly (CA) elected in 2013 is less inclusive than the first one and around 85% are new members. Thus, the CA Secretariat asked for capacity development to enable CA members to address the contentious issues of the first CA (2008-2012). A special attention will be given to empower representatives of excluded groups. UNDP SPCBN is a unique platform to strengthen inclusive and participatory processes in constitution-drafting and to foster consensus building.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Nepal, SET, Emergency Response Earthquake
rows.description: An earthquake of the magnitude of 7.8 hit Nepal and neighbouring countries in the early daylight hours of 25th April 2015. The result was substantial infrastructural damage across 30 out of 75 Nepali districts and a casualty toll rising to 1900 death by the 26the April 2015. Swiss Humanitarian Aid has taken the decision to deploy an interdisciplinary team consisting of expertise in WASH, medical response, shelter/structural engineering and logistics in order to support local and international actors in their efforts to respond to the emergency.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Nepal, Early Recovery and Expertise Reconstruction post-earthquake
rows.description: Immediately after the 25th of April 2015 earthquake in Nepal, the Humanitarian Aid of SDC deployed a rapid response team to the affected area consisting of experts in the field of shelter, medicine and water and sanitation. This intervention is followed by an early recovery and reconstruction program which focuses during eleven months on knowledge transfer on seismic sound reconstruction and the provision of access to remote and mountainous areas affected by the earthquake.

rows.reporting: Cordaid
rows.title: Support to Resilient Auto Recovery Rasuwa District Nepal
rows.description: After the devastating earthquake of 25 April 2015, Cordaid along with other Caritas organizations, provided emergency relief in Rasuwa and Gorkha, two districts heavily affected by the natural disaster. During the first six months, Cordaid focused on sustainable emergency aid. The program was then transferred to reconstruction with two local partners (Lumanti and Parivartan PATRA) and with two Village Development Committees (VDCs) from Rasuwa. In the reconstruction, much attention was paid to increasing the resilience of communities to better deal with future disasters. The aid was given directly to the people affected by the earthquake, through financial help for the houses, through training and through technical support in income activities, water and sanitation and housing construction. Cordaid was responsible for several projects, in addition to the activities carried out by Giro555 money.

rows.title: Support to Recovery and Reconstruction in Nepal Programme (SRRNP)
rows.description: Three major earthquakes left over 8500 people dead, 3 mio homeless and affected one third of the population in Nepal. Switzerlands contribution to the recovery and reconstruction efforts will ensure the equitable distribution of services and provisions especially from a gender and social inclusion perspective through, the provision of direct recovery and reconstruction support to the affected people, capacity building of the local government and other service delivery agencies.

rows.description: Incidence of poverty in irrigated areas of Nepal is half that in rain-fed areas and access to irrigation water contributes thus to mitigate poverty. Since the marginal lands are better accessible through small irrigation schemes, reducing poverty of smallholder farmers through small irrigation systems[1] are more effective than medium-sized or large systems. SIP will not only help increase their agricultural productivity but will also enhance the capacity of national and local government so that they can respond to these needs.[1] Small-scale irrigation, defined by the GoN's irrigation policy as command areas smaller than 25 ha in the hills and mountains and 200 ha in Terai.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Nepal Agricultural Services Development Programme (NASDP) – Main Credit Phase 1
rows.description: Agriculture in Nepal is characterized by low growth and productivity, yet it still is the mainstay for 66% of the rural poor people. The small farmers will improve their productivity, income and food security thanks to the strengthening of a decentralized and pluralistic extension and research system, receptive to their needs and demands. Built on previous SDC interventions and in line with Nepal’s Agricultural Development Strategy, this system will also enhance their social inclusion.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Governance Facility (GF)
rows.description: People in Nepal still suffer from weak public accountability, lack of rule of law and prevailing impunity. Switzerland, Denmark and DFID have established a sector-wide multi-donor basket facility to support public and private institutions in the governance sector, aiming at improving the rule of law, access to justice for marginalized men and women, the protection of human rights and at strengthening public accountability mechanisms for the most vulnerable people in Nepal.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: Enhanced Skills for Sustainable and Rewarding Employment (ENSSURE) (Main Credit)
rows.description: In Nepal, 450,000 youths enter the labour market yearly but remain un- or underemployed. Through ENSSURE, 21’000 youths will increase their employability by attending company-based trainings such as apprenticeships and further training of workers, combined with off-the-job systematic courses[1].The strong collaboration between the Ministry of Education and industry associations will increase the skill level of workers, thus labor productivity and will lead to more decent employment. [1] Systematic courses could be: Pre-employment basics, day or block release for related trade theory, pre-assessment course, etc.

rows.reporting: Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC)
rows.title: An array of measures to tackle gender-based violence
rows.description: Gender-based violence is a widespread problem in Nepal, and an obstacle to sustainable development. The SDC and the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) launched a 3-year prevention and response project at the beginning of 2016. Women and girls are learning about their rights and being encouraged to report assaults. Men and boys are being encouraged to change their behaviour towards women. And duty bearers are learning to respond more rapidly and effectively. The project is already delivering first encouraging results.

rows.reporting: Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development Canada (DFATD)
rows.title: Engaging Parliamentarians in Promoting Nuclear and Radiological Security, Phase II
rows.description: This project seeks to reduce the threat to global populations of a nuclear and radiological security event. This is done by promoting the ratification and implementation of the three key legal conventions that govern nuclear security. Project activities include: (1) the organization of ten parliamentary sessions to promote nuclear and radiological security in Cameroon, Grenada, Nepal, Sierra Leone, Seychelles, Suriname, Tanzania, The Gambia, Trinidad and Tobago, and Zimbabwe; (2) providing outreach and ongoing communications with participants; and (3) distributing a background briefing paper on the impact of nuclear and radiological weapons and materials on women...

rows.reporting: Cordaid
rows.title: Inclusive Hamro Coffee
rows.description:
The project Inclusive was developed to support the People With Disabilities for joining in the Coffee Value Chain. It is the continuation of Hamro Coffee as inclusive coffee value chain. The inclusive CVC task order is a Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) funded project and, it envisions being action-oriented and result based. The Light for the World (LFW) is leading this project whereas ICCO and NFDN are downstream implementing partners. The project design has driven an understanding of mitigating gaps among people with disabilities concerning their livelihood, stakeholders' participation, and engagement of Organizations for People with Disabilities (OPD) throughout the project lifecycle. The envisioned innovations will be implemented in two districts (Kavre and Sindhupalchok). The targeted for this innovation will be people with disabilities (physical, intellectual, mental, sensorial, pertaining to vision and hearing). The project focuses on working at different levels:
i)	Individual and family
ii)	Coffee Producer Organizations
iii)	Entrepreneurs/traders
iv)	Local /National service providers 
v)	System and governance. 

The domain of change across these different levels will be inter-linked with research, data and evidence which will be projected in form of the database management system of DID.

rows.reporting: Cordaid
rows.title: Change The Game Academy Nepal
rows.description:
Nepal Change the Game Academy Consortium (NCC) started its work in Nepal from November 2018. NCC consisted of the consortium of CSRC, ICCO Cooperation, Sahakarmi Samaj and Tewa. NCC was responsible for organizing a series of Change the Game Academy (CtgA) training in Nepal with the support from Wilde Ganzen. In the consortium, ICCO Nepal played a coordinating role to plan, implement and monitor the project. In December, 2018 ICCO Cooperation coordinated the Training of Trainers events for local civil society organizations with the help of Wilde Ganzen, MDF and Change the Game Academy. 12 A level trainers from Consortium participated in the training. Participants from India and Kenya were also there in the training program. The fundraising session was facilitated by Ger Robeling and Manju. Similarly, mobilizing support was facilitated by Ewoud Plate and Susan Njambi Odongo.
After preparing trainers to organize 3 Local Fundraising Training and 1 Mobilizing Support Training, the Nepal team did the contextualization of the training content which includes translation of training manual and literature. Then in 2019 and 2020, 4 classroom trainings were conducted along with refresher courses and several virtual coaching sessions. So far 81 participants are trained in Local Fundraising and 29 have been trained on Mobilization Support.
Apart from classroom training, Wilde Ganzen also supported the consortium during the Covid19 pandemic and helped in doing activities related to strengthening women's groups and enhancing livelihoods of marginalized communities. ICCO cooperation supported the Badi community in Kailali District, Sahakarmi Samaj supported marginalized communities in Dang and Bardiya. Similarly, CSRC supported farmers and daily wage workers in Mahottari District and Tewa also responded to the community with relief items, WASH activities and grant programs for their grantee partners. Total 1409 households were supported with relief materials.

rows.reporting: Cordaid
rows.title: ACT COVID-19 Nepal
rows.description:
Nepal is currently in the grips of the second wave of the COVID-19 pandemic. The second wave began from mid-April 2021, and daily infection rates have steadily been growing to a daily average of almost 9,000 per day from 300+ per day a month ago. As of 18th May 2021, a total of 5,411 deaths, 472,354 confirmed cases and 114,529 active cases have been recorded. The positivity rate, which is highest in the globe at this moment, is hovering around 45% nationally, with a transmission rate of 1.8 %.  

This rapid spike in cases can be attributed to several things such as relaxing of social distancing rules, reopening of schools, resuming mass gatherings for family functions, meetings, hosting political rallies and festivals, among others. However, a key factor has been the rise in Covid-19 cases, and the emergence of more infectious variants in neighbouring India. Nepal shares 1800 kms of open border with India; more than 50% of youth from Western Nepal work in India as seasonal migrants. With cases in India spiralling out of control, many migrant workers returned to their villages and towns in Nepal. Consequently, districts along the border with India such as Banke and Nepalgunj among others have become hotspots for the outbreak, along with Kathmandu, the most populous city in Nepal. 
 
Strain on the health care systems

Nepal’s dependence on India for most supplies, has further aggravated the crisis, as India prioritises its own needs first. Currently, Nepal is not only facing critical shortages in vaccines, but also immediate lifesaving medical supplies such as oxygen, medicine and other medical supplies. As per the GoN, there is 1 ICU bed per 17,930 people, 1 ventilator per 34,048 people, and 1 doctor per 1,740 people in Nepal. Lack of crucial infrastructure such as ICU beds and ventilators, combined with inadequate preparedness, poor diagnostic capacity, shortages in trained health care professionals, and squeeze in the medical supply chain have created a perfect storm.  
 
Economic and social consequences

Apart from public health consequences, the economic consequences of Covid are also alarming, especially for the workers in the informal sector and daily wage labourers. More than 50% of enterprises in Nepal are in the informal sector, with many affected by the prolonged lockdown in 2020, made worse by the ongoing lockdown orders, essentially bringing all economic activities to a standstill. World Bank last year stated that more than 1/3 of the population has been pushed below the poverty line by Covid. Lack of social protections, government schemes and assistance mean the most vulnerable are more at risk of falling into extreme poverty. Additionally, the crisis has also been linked to a rise in Gender Based violence and suicides.   
 
Government response to the crisis

The GoN has been very slow to respond to the crisis, with concrete steps being taken only more recently. However, the delay in preparedness and early response means that the country’s healthcare system has been completely overwhelmed by the uptick in cases. Significantly, the central government’s decision to ask local governments to fund and run their own quarantine/isolation centres meant that the quarantine facilities were ill-equipped and underfunded.  Rather than focusing on expanding the number of beds, securing oxygen supplies, ventilators, genomic sequencing machines, and vaccines with their own resources, the GoN’s early response was to rely on aid from international donors. Finally on May 11, Nepal’s Prime minister reached out to international community and directly requested support to help Nepal cope with the crisis. Similarly, Ministry of Health & Population, regulatory body of INGOs Social Welfare Council have urged development actors to support government initiatives of responding to covid crisis.

rows.reporting: United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO)
rows.title: ECW Fund: Disseminating life-saving messages through schools in flood-affected areas
rows.description: Beginning in the second week of August, Nepal experienced a period of sustained, heavy rainfall resulting in widespread flooding across 35 of the 75 districts. The number of affected schools has been reported to be 1866 (as of 15 Sept). More than 170,000 students have been affected and there is a crucial need of providing necessary support for children affected from the flood to help them overcome this kind of difficult experiences. Providing lifesaving messages and hygiene promotion for increased protection from various impacts of the disaster is to be carried out for affected children through teachers being trained in how to provide support to their students.
